title: Quiz: Future of Isogeny Cryptography
description: Test your understanding of emerging isogeny schemes

questions.question: What is SQIsign's main advantage over other PQC signatures?

options.text: Smallest combined public key + signature size

questions.explanation: SQIsign has the smallest signatures among PQC schemes (~177 bytes at Level 1 vs ~2,420 for Dilithium). However, it's much slower than lattice-based alternatives.

questions.question: Why is SQIsign not affected by the SIDH attack?

options.text: It's a signature scheme with different construction, no auxiliary torsion points
It was designed after the attack

questions.explanation: SQIsign uses quaternion algebra and endomorphism rings rather than the Diffie-Hellman style key exchange of SIDH. It doesn't publish auxiliary torsion points.

questions.question: What is CTIDH?
questions.explanation: CTIDH is a constant-time implementation of CSIDH that prevents timing attacks. It uses dummy operations to hide the real computation, making it slower but more secure.

questions.question: How much slower is SQIsign signing compared to Dilithium?
questions.explanation: SQIsign signing takes 200-1000ms compared to ~0.3ms for Dilithium - roughly 700-3000× slower. This is the main drawback of isogeny-based signatures.

questions.question: Which isogeny scheme is currently in NIST's 'additional signatures' round?

options.text: None - isogeny signatures are all broken

questions.explanation: SQIsign was submitted to NIST's call for additional digital signature algorithms and is currently being evaluated. It was not broken by the SIDH attack.

questions.question: What is the current recommended approach for most PQC deployments?

options.text: Use isogeny-based schemes for smallest keys
Use NIST standards (ML-KEM, ML-DSA) for most applications
Wait for isogeny schemes to be standardized
Use classical crypto with larger keys

questions.explanation: The recommended approach is to use NIST-standardized schemes (ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA) for most applications. Isogeny schemes are for niche use cases with extreme size constraints.

questions.question: The SIDH attack completely ended all isogeny-based cryptography research.
questions.explanation: False! While SIDH is broken, CSIDH survives, SQIsign is promising, and new schemes like SCALLOP are being developed. Isogeny crypto continues as an active research area.

questions.question: SQIsign is well-suited for high-volume server authentication.
questions.explanation: False! SQIsign is very slow (signing takes 200-1000ms) making it unsuitable for high-volume scenarios. It's better for certificate chains or firmware signing where size matters more than speed.
